title: Android Fundamentals
description: Core components, lifecycle, configuration changes, resource system, source sets, drawable density, View layout (margin/padding/width/height), RTL, multi-screen, and lifecycle debugging

references.title: Android Developers — How Android Finds the Best Resource
Android Developers — Support Different Screen Sizes

lesson:
The Android Application Model

Android apps are built from four core components — Activity, Service, BroadcastReceiver, and ContentProvider. Unlike web apps with a single entry point, Android apps have multiple entry points.

ComponentPurposeManaged ByLifetime
ActivitySingle screen with UIActivityManagerServiceUntil finished or system destroys
ServiceBackground work, no UIActiveServices (part of AMS)Until stopped or killed
BroadcastReceiverResponds to system eventsBroadcastQueue (part of AMS)Duration of onReceive() only
ContentProviderShared data interfaceContentProviderManagerWhile in use by clients

Activity Lifecycle — The Full Picture

onCreate() → onStart() → onResume() [RUNNING — visible & interactive] onPause() ← user partially obscured (dialog, multi-window) onStop() ← fully hidden (home button, new activity) onDestroy() ← finishing or system reclaiming memory Rotation: onPause → onStop → onDestroy → onCreate → onStart → onResume (entire Activity recreated! ViewModel survives this) Process death: onStop → [killed] → onCreate (with savedInstanceState) ViewModel is GONE. Only SavedStateHandle survives.
CallbackStateWhat To Do
onCreate()Created, not visibleInflate layout, set up ViewModel, bind data. Called with savedInstanceState Bundle if restoring.
onStart()Visible, not interactiveRegister UI-related observers (LiveData, Flow collection via repeatOnLifecycle)
onResume()Foreground, interactiveStart camera, resume animations, acquire exclusive resources
onPause()Partially visibleRelease exclusive resources (camera). Do NOT save data here — onPause can be very brief.
onStop()HiddenRelease heavy resources. Save transient data. Unregister non-lifecycle-aware listeners.
onDestroy()Being destroyedClean up. Check isFinishing to distinguish finish() from config change.
Multi-window lifecycle: On API 29+, multiple Activities can be in RESUMED state simultaneously (multi-resume). Use onTopResumedActivityChanged() to know if you're the topmost resumed Activity — this is where you should acquire exclusive resources like the camera.

Configuration Changes & Activity Recreation

A configuration change is any change to the device's runtime configuration — screen rotation, language switch, font size change, dark mode toggle, keyboard availability, screen resize (multi-window). By default, Android destroys and recreates the Activity to pick up new resources.

User rotates phone │ ▼ System detects Configuration change │ ├── Activity.onPause() ├── Activity.onStop() ├── Activity.onSaveInstanceState(bundle) ← save transient UI state ├── Activity.onDestroy() ← old Activity gone │ ├── Activity.onCreate(savedInstanceState) ← NEW Activity with new config ├── Activity.onStart() └── Activity.onResume() What survives: ✓ ViewModel (config changes only, NOT process death) ✓ SavedInstanceState Bundle (config changes AND process death) ✗ Plain fields, lateinit vars, running coroutines (without viewModelScope)

android:configChanges — Opting Out

You can declare android:configChanges in the manifest to handle certain config changes yourself without recreation:

// Manifest: handle orientation and screen size yourself <activity android:name=".VideoPlayerActivity" android:configChanges="orientation|screenSize|smallestScreenSize" /> // Activity: system calls this instead of recreating override fun onConfigurationChanged(newConfig: Configuration) { super.onConfigurationChanged(newConfig) if (newConfig.orientation == Configuration.ORIENTATION_LANDSCAPE) { // Adjust layout without full recreation } }
When to use configChanges: Almost never. It's tempting to avoid recreation, but you lose automatic resource re-selection (layout-land, values-night, etc.). Only use it for Activities that must maintain continuous state, like video players or OpenGL views. For everything else, let the system recreate and use ViewModel + SavedStateHandle to restore state.
Config ChangeTriggers Recreation?Common Cause
RotationYesUser rotates device
LocaleYesUser changes language in Settings
UI mode (dark/light)YesDark mode toggle
Font scaleYesUser changes display size / font size
Screen sizeYesMulti-window: entering/exiting split screen
Keyboard hidden/shownDepends on manifestHardware keyboard attached/detached

The Resource System & Qualifier Matching

Android's resource system is how your app adapts to different devices, languages, screen sizes, and configurations without writing conditional code. Resources live in res/ subdirectories with qualifier suffixes.

res/ ├── drawable/ ← default drawables ├── drawable-mdpi/ ← medium density (~160dpi) ├── drawable-hdpi/ ← high density (~240dpi) ├── drawable-xhdpi/ ← extra-high density (~320dpi) ├── drawable-xxhdpi/ ← extra-extra-high (~480dpi) ├── drawable-xxxhdpi/ ← extra-extra-extra-high (~640dpi) ├── layout/ ← default layout ├── layout-land/ ← landscape orientation ├── layout-sw600dp/ ← smallest width ≥ 600dp (tablets) ├── layout-w900dp/ ← current width ≥ 900dp ├── values/ ← default strings, dimens, colors ├── values-es/ ← Spanish strings ├── values-ja/ ← Japanese strings ├── values-night/ ← dark mode colors/themes ├── values-v31/ ← API 31+ specific values └── values-sw600dp-land/ ← tablet + landscape (multiple qualifiers)

How Android Finds the Best-Matching Resource

When your code references R.drawable.icon, the system uses an elimination algorithm:

Step 1: Eliminate qualifier groups that contradict the device config Device is: en-US, portrait, xhdpi, sw411dp, API 34, night mode drawable/ ← keep (no qualifiers = universal) drawable-mdpi/ ← eliminate (density doesn't match) drawable-hdpi/ ← eliminate drawable-xhdpi/ ← keep (matches!) drawable-xxhdpi/ ← keep for now (density can scale down) drawable-land/ ← eliminate (not landscape) Step 2: Walk through qualifier precedence order (MCC → language → layout direction → smallest width → width → height → screen size → orientation → UI mode → night → density → ... → API level) At each step, eliminate dirs that don't match the highest-priority qualifier. Step 3: Last directory standing wins. If only default (no qualifier) remains, use that.
Density is special: If the exact density bucket doesn't exist, Android picks the next higher density and scales down (preferred) or the next lower and scales up. Scaling up is worse — it produces blurry images. Always provide xxhdpi at minimum, since most modern phones are xxhdpi or xxxhdpi. Or use vector drawables which scale perfectly.
Multiple qualifiers: You can combine qualifiers: values-es-night matches Spanish + dark mode. Qualifiers must follow the fixed precedence order — values-night-es is invalid (night must come before language? Actually language comes before night — the order matters!).

Source Sets

Source sets control which code and resources are included in a particular build. They layer on top of each other:

Build: freeDebug main/ ← always included (your primary code + resources) ├── java/ ├── res/ └── AndroidManifest.xml debug/ ← included only in debug builds ├── java/ (can add classes, NOT override main) └── res/ (resources OVERLAY main — same name replaces) free/ ← included only in 'free' flavor ├── java/ └── res/ freeDebug/ ← included only in free + debug combination └── res/ Overlay priority (highest wins for resources): freeDebug > free > debug > main For Java/Kotlin: source sets are ADDITIVE (no overriding) For resources: same-name resources in higher-priority sets REPLACE lower ones For manifests: merged with manifest merger (higher priority wins conflicts)
Source SetWhen IncludedUse Case
mainAlwaysCore app code and resources
debugDebug buildsDebug tools (LeakCanary, Flipper), test API URLs
releaseRelease buildsProduction API URLs, analytics config
{flavor}That flavor onlyFree vs paid features, different branding
testLocal unit testsTest doubles, test data
androidTestInstrumented testsUI test helpers, test rules

Drawable Density & Image Scaling

Android screens have different pixel densities (DPI). To make images look sharp on all devices, you provide different sizes in density-qualified drawable directories:

Density BucketDPIScale Factor48dp icon =
mdpi~1601x (baseline)48×48 px
hdpi~2401.5x72×72 px
xhdpi~3202x96×96 px
xxhdpi~4803x144×144 px
xxxhdpi~6404x192×192 px
// dp (density-independent pixels) → actual pixels px = dp × (dpi / 160) // On a 320dpi (xhdpi) screen: 48dp × (320/160) = 48 × 2 = 96px // On a 480dpi (xxhdpi) screen: 48dp × (480/160) = 48 × 3 = 144px

What Happens When a Density Bucket Is Missing?

Device is xxhdpi (480dpi). Looking for R.drawable.photo: 1. Check drawable-xxhdpi/ → found? Use it ✓ 2. Not found? Check drawable-xxxhdpi/ → scale DOWN (preferred) 3. Not found? Check drawable-xhdpi/ → scale UP (blurry!) 4. Not found? Check drawable-hdpi/ → scale UP more (blurrier!) 5. Last resort: drawable/ (no qualifier) → treated as mdpi, scaled up 3x Always scales from nearest. Higher density is preferred over lower.
Vector drawables (.xml) scale to any size without quality loss. Use them for icons, logos, and simple graphics. For photos and complex images, provide raster assets in at least xxhdpi. Use WebP format over PNG for 25-35% smaller file sizes.

View Layout Fundamentals: Size, Margin & Padding

Every View has a rectangular area defined by width, height, margin (outside), and padding (inside). Understanding the box model is critical for debugging layout issues on real devices.

┌──────────────── Margin ────────────────┐ │ │ │ ┌──────────── Border ────────────┐ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ ┌──── Padding ────┐ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ CONTENT │ │ │ │ │ │ (text, image) │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ └─────────────────┘ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ └────────────────────────────────┘ │ │ │ └────────────────────────────────────────┘ Margin: space OUTSIDE the view (between this view and neighbors) Padding: space INSIDE the view (between border and content) Background: fills the padding area but NOT margin

Width & Height

ValueMeaningBehavior
match_parentFill parent's available spaceView expands to parent's width/height minus parent's padding
wrap_contentShrink to fit contentView measures its content and becomes just big enough
Fixed dp (e.g. 200dp)Exact sizeAlways this size regardless of content or parent
0dp (in ConstraintLayout)Match constraintsFills the space defined by constraints
0dp (in LinearLayout with weight)Weighted distributionRemaining space distributed by layout_weight

layout_weight in LinearLayout

// Two buttons splitting width equally <LinearLayout android:orientation="horizontal" ...> <Button android:layout_width="0dp" android:layout_height="wrap_content" android:layout_weight="1" /> // gets 1/(1+1) = 50% <Button android:layout_width="0dp" android:layout_height="wrap_content" android:layout_weight="1" /> // gets 1/(1+1) = 50% </LinearLayout> // WRONG: using match_parent with weight — causes double-measurement! // The view is first measured at match_parent, then the weight is applied. // Use 0dp to avoid this performance hit ("double taxation").

gravity vs layout_gravity

AttributeApplied ToWhat It Does
android:gravityThe View itselfPositions the View's content within its own bounds (like text alignment)
android:layout_gravityThe View's parentPositions the View within its parent's remaining space
Think of it this way: gravity = "where does my content sit inside me?" layout_gravity = "where do I sit inside my parent?" In Compose, these map to Alignment on containers and Modifier.align() on children.

RTL Support & Layout Direction

Languages like Arabic, Hebrew, Farsi, and Urdu read right-to-left (RTL). Android can mirror your entire layout automatically, but you must use direction-neutral attributes:

OLD (DO NOT USE)NEW (USE THIS)Why
paddingLeftpaddingStartStart = left in LTR, right in RTL
paddingRightpaddingEndEnd = right in LTR, left in RTL
marginLeftmarginStartAuto-mirrors for RTL
marginRightmarginEndAuto-mirrors for RTL
layout_alignParentLeftlayout_alignParentStartRelativeLayout auto-mirrors
gravity="left"gravity="start"Text and content alignment
// Enable RTL support in manifest <application android:supportsRtl="true" ...> // Layout: use start/end instead of left/right <TextView android:layout_width="match_parent" android:layout_height="wrap_content" android:paddingStart="16dp" android:paddingEnd="8dp" android:drawableStart="@drawable/ic_arrow" android:gravity="start" />
Common RTL bugs:
• Using left/right instead of start/end — layout doesn't mirror
• Hardcoded drawable arrows pointing → — should flip in RTL
• Progress bars filling left-to-right — should reverse in RTL
• Testing tip: Toggle Force RTL layout direction in Developer Options to test without changing language

Multi-Screen & Display Adaptation

Android runs on phones, tablets, foldables, TVs, cars, watches, and Chromebooks. Your app must handle wildly different screen sizes:

QualifierMeaningExample
sw600dpSmallest width ≥ 600dp7" tablets and larger
sw720dpSmallest width ≥ 720dp10" tablets
w600dpCurrent available width ≥ 600dpLandscape phone, portrait tablet
h480dpCurrent available height ≥ 480dpRarely used
land / portOrientationLandscape vs portrait layouts

sw (smallest width) = the shortest side of the screen, regardless of orientation. A 7" tablet has sw~600dp whether portrait or landscape. This makes it the most reliable qualifier for phone vs tablet distinction.

// res/layout/activity_main.xml — phone: single pane <FrameLayout> <fragment android:id="@+id/list_fragment" /> </FrameLayout> // res/layout-sw600dp/activity_main.xml — tablet: two-pane <LinearLayout android:orientation="horizontal"> <fragment android:id="@+id/list_fragment" android:layout_width="0dp" android:layout_weight="1" /> <fragment android:id="@+id/detail_fragment" android:layout_width="0dp" android:layout_weight="2" /> </LinearLayout>
Multi-window window size ≠ display size: In split-screen mode, your app's window is smaller than the screen. Use WindowMetricsCalculator (Jetpack WindowManager) to get your actual window size, NOT Display.getSize(). In Compose, use WindowSizeClass to reactively adapt layout to compact/medium/expanded width.

Fragment Lifecycle

Fragments have their own lifecycle that is tied to but separate from the hosting Activity's lifecycle. Fragments also have a View lifecycle which is shorter than the Fragment lifecycle:

Fragment Lifecycle (attached to Activity) onAttach() └── onCreate() ← Fragment created (no view yet) └── onCreateView() ← Inflate layout, return root view └── onViewCreated() ← View exists! Set up UI here └── onStart() ← Visible └── onResume() ← Interactive onPause() ← Partially obscured onStop() ← Hidden onDestroyView() ← VIEW destroyed (Fragment still alive!) onDestroy() ← Fragment destroyed onDetach() ← Removed from Activity Key insight: Fragment can outlive its View! Navigation: When you navigate forward, the Fragment stays in the back stack (alive!) but its View is destroyed (onDestroyView). Navigate back → onCreateView runs again with a NEW View.
Fragment View Lifecycle trap: If you observe LiveData or collect Flow in onCreateView(), you must use viewLifecycleOwner — not this (the Fragment). The Fragment outlives its View, so using this causes updates to a destroyed View and memory leaks.

viewLifecycleOwner.lifecycleScope.launch { ... } — coroutine dies with the View
this.lifecycleScope.launch { ... } — coroutine lives with the Fragment (bad for UI work)

View Lifecycle (Attach, Measure, Layout, Draw)

Views have their own lifecycle independent of Activity/Fragment, driven by the window system:

View Lifecycle constructor() ← View object created (from XML inflation or code) └── onFinishInflate() ← All children from XML are inflated └── onAttachedToWindow() ← View added to a window (has Surface) └── onMeasure() ← Parent asks: how big are you? └── onLayout() ← Parent says: here's your position └── onDraw() ← Draw yourself on the Canvas ... user interacts, state changes ... onDetachedFromWindow() ← View removed from window Release resources here (stop animations, unregister listeners, cancel ongoing work)
CallbackWhenDo
onAttachedToWindowView added to window hierarchyStart animations, register observers
onDetachedFromWindowView removed from windowStop animations, release resources, cancel work
onMeasureParent needs to know your sizeCalculate desired width/height from content + MeasureSpec
onSizeChangedSize changed after measure/layoutRecalculate drawing coordinates, recreate bitmaps
onDrawTime to renderCanvas drawing operations. Keep fast — runs every frame!

Debugging Lifecycle Issues

Lifecycle bugs are the most common source of crashes and unexpected behavior on real devices. Here's how to find them:

ToolWhat It ShowsUse For
Logcat lifecycle filterLog each lifecycle callbackVerify order and frequency of callbacks
StrictModePolicy violations on main threadDetect disk/network IO on UI thread
Layout InspectorLive View hierarchy snapshotDebug layout sizes, margins, padding on device
Don't Keep ActivitiesDeveloper option that destroys every Activity on leaveSimulate aggressive process death to find state-loss bugs
Force RTLDeveloper option to mirror all layoutsFind hardcoded left/right layout issues
Smallest width overrideDeveloper option to simulate different screen sizesTest tablet layouts on a phone
// Add lifecycle logging to every Activity class BaseActivity : AppCompatActivity() { override fun onCreate(s: Bundle?) { super.onCreate(s) Log.d("Lifecycle", "${this::class.simpleName}.onCreate") } override fun onStart() { super.onStart() Log.d("Lifecycle", "${this::class.simpleName}.onStart") } override fun onResume() { super.onResume() Log.d("Lifecycle", "${this::class.simpleName}.onResume") } override fun onPause() { super.onPause() Log.d("Lifecycle", "${this::class.simpleName}.onPause") } override fun onStop() { super.onStop() Log.d("Lifecycle", "${this::class.simpleName}.onStop") } override fun onDestroy() { super.onDestroy() Log.d("Lifecycle", "${this::class.simpleName}.onDestroy isFinishing=$isFinishing") } override fun onConfigurationChanged(c: Configuration) { super.onConfigurationChanged(c) Log.d("Lifecycle", "${this::class.simpleName}.onConfigurationChanged") } }
The #1 lifecycle debugging technique: Enable "Don't Keep Activities" in Developer Options. This destroys your Activity the moment you leave it — simulating what happens when the system is under memory pressure. If your app loses state, your savedInstanceState or SavedStateHandle handling is wrong. Every professional Android developer should test with this enabled regularly.

How Intents Work — The System Path

When you call startActivity(intent), the request travels via Binder IPC to ActivityManagerService in the system_server process:

Your App Process system_server Process ───────────────── ──────────────────── startActivity(intent) │ └──► Binder IPC ──────────────► ActivityManagerService (AMS) │ ├── resolveIntent() │ └── PackageManagerService │ └── scans all apps' │ AndroidManifest.xml │ for matching intent-filters ├── Check permissions ├── Find or create target Task ├── Find or create target Process │ └── Zygote forks if needed └── Send to target app │ Target App ◄──────────────────────────────────┘ ActivityThread.handleLaunchActivity() └── activity.onCreate(bundle)
  • Explicit Intent — specifies exact target: Intent(this, DetailActivity::class.java)
  • Implicit Intent — declares an action: Intent(ACTION_SEND). PMS scans all manifests for matching <intent-filter>. Multiple matches → chooser dialog

Tasks vs Activities vs Processes

These are three separate concepts that are commonly confused:

Process (Linux process — one per app, has PID and memory) └── Application (singleton, global state) └── ActivityThread (main/UI thread) Task (logical back stack — can span processes!) └── Activity A (bottom of stack) └── Activity B └── Activity C (top — foreground, visible)
Key insight: A Task is NOT a process. Tasks can contain Activities from different apps/processes. When your app launches the Camera app, the Camera Activity joins YOUR task's back stack.

Launch Modes

launchModeBehavior
standard (default)New instance every time
singleTopIf on TOP → reuse + onNewIntent(). Otherwise → new instance.
singleTaskOne instance per task; clears above it when re-activated.
singleInstanceAlone in its task. No other Activity can join.

Window Management & DecorView

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ SurfaceFlinger │ │ (composites all surfaces into final frame) │ ├──────────┬──────────────────┬────────────────────┤ │ StatusBar│ App Surface │ NavigationBar │ │ (SystemUI│ (DecorView) │ (SystemUI) │ │ window) │ │ │ └──────────┴──────────────────┴────────────────────┘ DecorView (in YOUR app process) └── Root View of every Activity window └── PhoneWindow creates it └── Your setContentView() layout goes inside

questions.title: The Four Components
questions.description: What are the 4 fundamental building blocks of an Android application?
options: Activity, Fragment, Service, Intent
Activity, Service, BroadcastReceiver, ContentProvider
Activity, Service, ViewModel, Repository
Activity, Fragment, BroadcastReceiver, ContentProvider
questions.explanation: The four fundamental Android components are: Activity (UI screens), Service (background operations), BroadcastReceiver (responds to system-wide events), and ContentProvider (manages shared data). Fragments are UI components within Activities, not standalone components. Intents are messaging objects, not components.
hints: Think about what you register in AndroidManifest.xml
Fragments live inside Activities — they are not top-level components

questions.title: Activity Lifecycle
questions.description: Which lifecycle callback is called when an Activity first becomes visible to the user (but may not yet be in the foreground)?
options: onCreate()
onStart()
onResume()
onVisible()
questions.explanation: onCreate() initializes the Activity but it is not yet visible. onStart() is called when the Activity becomes visible. onResume() is called when it moves to the foreground and becomes interactive. onVisible() does not exist in the Android lifecycle.
hints: The lifecycle order is: onCreate → onStart → onResume
Visible and interactive are two different states

questions.title: Creating an Intent
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to navigate from the current Activity to DetailActivity, passing an item ID.
questions.code:
// Navigate to DetailActivity
val intent = ___1___(this, DetailActivity::class.java)
intent.putExtra("ITEM_ID", itemId)
___2___(intent)

blanks.hint: Method to launch the new Activity

questions.explanation: An Intent is created with the current context and target Activity class. startActivity(intent) launches the target Activity. Data is passed via putExtra() key-value pairs.
hints: The class that represents a messaging object in Android
The Activity method that launches another Activity

questions.title: What is an Intent?
questions.description: In your own words, explain what an Intent is in Android and give an example of when you would use one.
questions.answer: An Intent is a messaging object used to request an action from another app component. It can be used to start an Activity, start a Service, or deliver a broadcast. For example, you use an explicit Intent to navigate between screens in your app, or an implicit Intent to open a URL in the browser.
questions.explanation: Intents are the primary mechanism for inter-component communication in Android. Explicit intents specify the exact component to start. Implicit intents declare a general action, letting the system find a suitable component.
hints: Think about how different parts of an Android app communicate
There are two types: explicit and implicit

questions.title: Missing Permission
questions.description: This app crashes with a SecurityException when trying to fetch data from the internet. Fix the AndroidManifest.xml.
questions.starterCode:
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<manifest xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android">

    <application
        android:allowBackup="true"
        android:label="@string/app_name"
        android:theme="@style/Theme.MyApp">

        <activity
            android:name=".MainActivity"
            android:exported="true">
            <intent-filter>
                <action android:name="android.intent.action.MAIN" />
                <category android:name="android.intent.category.LAUNCHER" />
            </intent-filter>
        </activity>

    </application>

</manifest>
questions.solution:
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<manifest xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android">

    <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.INTERNET" />

    <application
        android:allowBackup="true"
        android:label="@string/app_name"
        android:theme="@style/Theme.MyApp">

        <activity
            android:name=".MainActivity"
            android:exported="true">
            <intent-filter>
                <action android:name="android.intent.action.MAIN" />
                <category android:name="android.intent.category.LAUNCHER" />
            </intent-filter>
        </activity>

    </application>

</manifest>
questions.explanation: Android requires explicit permission declarations. The <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.INTERNET" /> tag must be added before <application>.
hints: What XML tag declares permissions in AndroidManifest.xml?
The permission should go between <manifest> and <application>

questions.title: AndroidManifest.xml
questions.description: What is the primary purpose of the AndroidManifest.xml file?
options: To define the app's visual theme and styling
To declare app components, permissions, and metadata to the Android system
To configure build settings like minSdk and dependencies
To store string resources for localization
questions.explanation: The AndroidManifest.xml is the app's identity card to the Android system. It declares all components (Activities, Services, etc.), required permissions, hardware features, and app metadata. The system reads it to know what your app contains and what it needs. Build settings go in build.gradle, themes in themes.xml, and strings in strings.xml.
hints: Think about what the system needs to know about your app before launching it

questions.title: Implicit Intent
questions.description: What happens when this code runs?
questions.code:
val sendIntent = Intent().apply {
    action = Intent.ACTION_SEND
    putExtra(Intent.EXTRA_TEXT, "Check out this app!")
    type = "text/plain"
}
val chooser = Intent.createChooser(sendIntent, "Share via")
startActivity(chooser)
options: It opens a specific messaging app and sends the text
It shows a system dialog letting the user choose which app to share text with
It sends an SMS message to all contacts
It crashes because no target Activity is specified
questions.explanation: This creates an implicit intent with ACTION_SEND — it doesn't specify a target app. createChooser() wraps it in a chooser dialog that shows all apps capable of handling text/plain sharing (email, messaging, social media, etc.). The user picks which app to use.
hints: No specific Activity class is mentioned — how does the system know where to send it?
createChooser shows a selection UI

questions.title: Registering a BroadcastReceiver
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to register a BroadcastReceiver that listens for airplane mode changes.

blanks.hint: The base class for broadcast listeners
The callback method when a broadcast arrives

questions.explanation: BroadcastReceiver is the base class. onReceive() is called when a matching broadcast arrives. IntentFilter specifies which broadcasts to listen for. Remember to unregisterReceiver() in onDestroy() to prevent leaks.
hints: The class extends a specific Android component type
The filter tells the system which broadcasts you care about

questions.title: Activity Not in Manifest
questions.description: The app crashes with "ActivityNotFoundException" when navigating to SettingsActivity. The Activity class exists and compiles fine. Fix it.
questions.starterCode:
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<manifest xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android">
    <application
        android:label="@string/app_name"
        android:theme="@style/Theme.MyApp">

        <activity
            android:name=".MainActivity"
            android:exported="true">
            <intent-filter>
                <action android:name="android.intent.action.MAIN" />
                <category android:name="android.intent.category.LAUNCHER" />
            </intent-filter>
        </activity>

        <!-- SettingsActivity is missing! -->

    </application>
</manifest>
questions.solution:
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<manifest xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android">
    <application
        android:label="@string/app_name"
        android:theme="@style/Theme.MyApp">

        <activity
            android:name=".MainActivity"
            android:exported="true">
            <intent-filter>
                <action android:name="android.intent.action.MAIN" />
                <category android:name="android.intent.category.LAUNCHER" />
            </intent-filter>
        </activity>

        <activity android:name=".SettingsActivity" />

    </application>
</manifest>
questions.explanation: Every Activity must be declared in AndroidManifest.xml. Even though the class compiles, the Android system doesn't know about it without a manifest entry.
hints: All Android components must be declared somewhere the system can find them
Check what's missing between the application tags

questions.title: Configuration Change Behavior
questions.description: The user rotates their phone while filling out a form in your Activity. What happens by default (without any special handling)?
options: The Activity pauses and resumes — nothing is lost
The Activity is destroyed and recreated. ViewModel state survives but plain member variables are reset to defaults.
The Activity is destroyed and recreated. All state including ViewModel is lost.
The system asks the user if they want to restart the Activity
questions.explanation: On rotation, the system destroys the Activity (onPause → onStop → onSaveInstanceState → onDestroy) and recreates it (onCreate with savedInstanceState → onStart → onResume). ViewModel survives configuration changes (it's stored in ViewModelStore, which is retained). But plain fields (var count = 0) are reset. SavedStateHandle survives both config changes AND process death.
hints: The system needs to reload resources for the new configuration (layout-land, etc.)
ViewModel was specifically designed to survive this scenario

questions.title: Resource Qualifier Matching
questions.description: Your app has these resource directories. The device is a Pixel 7 with: English (US), portrait orientation, xxhdpi (480dpi), sw411dp, API 34, dark mode. Which strings.xml is used?
questions.code:
res/
├── values/strings.xml            // default
├── values-es/strings.xml         // Spanish
├── values-night/strings.xml      // dark mode
├── values-en-night/strings.xml   // English + dark mode
├── values-v31/strings.xml        // API 31+
├── values-sw600dp/strings.xml    // tablet
options: values/strings.xml — the default always wins when multiple match
values-night/strings.xml — dark mode is the most specific qualifier
values-en-night/strings.xml — matches both language (en) and UI mode (night), most specific
values-v31/strings.xml — API version has highest priority
questions.explanation: The system eliminates non-matching directories: values-es (wrong language), values-sw600dp (phone is only 411dp). Then it walks the qualifier precedence order. Language has higher precedence than UI mode or API level. values-en-night matches English (the device locale) AND dark mode — it's the most specific match. values-v31 matches but is less specific because language qualifiers take precedence over API version.
hints: Language/locale has very high priority in the qualifier precedence order
More qualifiers = more specific = wins when all match

learnMore.title: Android Developers — How the Best Resource Is Found

questions.title: Drawable Density Selection
questions.description: Your app provides drawable-hdpi/icon.png and drawable-xxxhdpi/icon.png, but NOT xhdpi or xxhdpi. The device is xxhdpi (480dpi). What happens?
options: The app crashes because drawable-xxhdpi is missing
Android uses drawable-hdpi and scales it UP to xxhdpi size (blurry)
Android uses drawable-xxxhdpi and scales it DOWN to xxhdpi size (sharp, slightly more memory)
Android uses the default drawable/ directory
questions.explanation: When the exact density bucket is missing, Android prefers the next higher density and scales down. Scaling down preserves quality (sharp). Scaling up produces blurry results. So drawable-xxxhdpi (640dpi → scaled to 480dpi) is preferred over drawable-hdpi (240dpi → scaled up to 480dpi). This is why you should always provide at least xxhdpi assets — or better, use vector drawables.
hints: Think about image quality: is scaling up or scaling down better?
The system picks the closest density that avoids enlarging the image

questions.title: View Layout: Margin & Padding
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to create a TextView with 16dp inner spacing, 8dp outer spacing from siblings, that fills the parent width and wraps its content height.
questions.code:
<TextView
    android:layout_width="___1___"
    android:layout_height="___2___"
    android:___3___="16dp"
    android:___4___="8dp"
    android:text="Hello World" />
questions.explanation: match_parent fills the parent's available space. wrap_content sizes the View to fit its content. padding adds space inside the View (between border and content — background fills this area). layout_margin adds space outside the View (between this View and its neighbors).
hints: Inner spacing = padding, outer spacing = margin
The width fills the parent, the height adjusts to content

questions.title: RTL Layout Bug
questions.description: This layout looks correct in English (LTR) but the icon and text overlap in Arabic (RTL). The icon should be at the leading edge and text should have spacing from it. Fix the hardcoded left/right attributes.
questions.starterCode:
<LinearLayout
    android:layout_width="match_parent"
    android:layout_height="wrap_content"
    android:orientation="horizontal"
    android:paddingLeft="16dp"
    android:paddingRight="16dp">

    <ImageView
        android:layout_width="24dp"
        android:layout_height="24dp"
        android:src="@drawable/ic_info"
        android:layout_gravity="center_vertical" />

    <TextView
        android:layout_width="wrap_content"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:paddingLeft="12dp"
        android:text="@string/info_text"
        android:gravity="left" />

</LinearLayout>
questions.solution:
<LinearLayout
    android:layout_width="match_parent"
    android:layout_height="wrap_content"
    android:orientation="horizontal"
    android:paddingStart="16dp"
    android:paddingEnd="16dp">

    <ImageView
        android:layout_width="24dp"
        android:layout_height="24dp"
        android:src="@drawable/ic_info"
        android:layout_gravity="center_vertical" />

    <TextView
        android:layout_width="wrap_content"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:paddingStart="12dp"
        android:text="@string/info_text"
        android:gravity="start" />

</LinearLayout>
questions.explanation: In RTL languages, left and right are absolute — they don't flip. start and end are direction-aware: start = left in LTR, right in RTL. Replace all paddingLeft → paddingStart, paddingRight → paddingEnd, gravity="left" → gravity="start". Also ensure android:supportsRtl="true" is in your manifest.
hints: In Arabic, the 'start' of a line is the RIGHT side
Replace left/right with start/end throughout

questions.title: Fragment Lifecycle vs Activity
questions.description: A Fragment is displayed inside an Activity. The user presses the Home button. Which lifecycle callbacks run and in what order?
options: Activity.onPause → Activity.onStop (Fragment callbacks don't run)
Fragment.onPause → Activity.onPause → Fragment.onStop → Activity.onStop
Activity.onPause → Fragment.onPause → Activity.onStop → Fragment.onStop
Fragment.onPause → Fragment.onStop → Activity.onPause → Activity.onStop
questions.explanation: When the Activity is pausing, the Activity's callback runs first, then the Fragment's. The order is: Activity.onPause() → Fragment.onPause() → Activity.onStop() → Fragment.onStop(). The Activity drives the lifecycle — Fragments follow. On the way back up (resume), it's reversed: Fragment.onStart → Activity.onStart → Fragment.onResume → Activity.onResume.
hints: The Activity is the parent — it drives the lifecycle
Fragment lifecycle callbacks run after the corresponding Activity callback

questions.title: configChanges Attribute
questions.description: What happens when the user rotates their phone while this Activity is displayed?
questions.code:
<!-- AndroidManifest.xml -->
<activity
    android:name=".VideoPlayerActivity"
    android:configChanges="orientation|screenSize" />

// In VideoPlayerActivity:
override fun onConfigurationChanged(newConfig: Configuration) {
    super.onConfigurationChanged(newConfig)
    if (newConfig.orientation == Configuration.ORIENTATION_LANDSCAPE) {
        enterFullScreen()
    } else {
        exitFullScreen()
    }
}
options: The Activity is destroyed and recreated normally — configChanges has no effect
The Activity is NOT destroyed. Instead, onConfigurationChanged() is called, and the Activity switches to/from fullscreen without interrupting video playback
The Activity crashes because you cannot handle orientation changes manually
The Activity is recreated, but onConfigurationChanged is also called before recreation
questions.explanation: Declaring android:configChanges="orientation|screenSize" tells the system: "I'll handle rotation myself." The Activity is not destroyed or recreated. Instead, onConfigurationChanged() is called with the new Configuration. This is ideal for video players — playback continues uninterrupted. The tradeoff: you lose automatic resource re-selection (e.g., layout-land is not automatically loaded).
hints: configChanges is specifically designed to prevent Activity recreation
Video players use this to avoid interrupting playback on rotation

questions.title: match_parent vs wrap_content vs 0dp with weight
questions.description: In a horizontal LinearLayout, you want two Buttons to each take exactly half the width. Which setup is correct and most efficient?
options: Both buttons: layout_width="match_parent" with layout_weight="1"
Both buttons: layout_width="wrap_content" with layout_weight="1"
Both buttons: layout_width="0dp" with layout_weight="1"
Both buttons: layout_width="50%" (percentage is supported)
questions.explanation: layout_width="0dp" with layout_weight="1" is the correct and most efficient approach. Setting width to 0dp tells LinearLayout to skip the initial measurement and go directly to weight-based distribution. Using match_parent with weight causes double measurement — each child is first measured at full width, then remeasured to distribute by weight. Using wrap_content with weight is unpredictable because remaining space depends on content size. Android does not support percentage-based widths in LinearLayout.
hints: 0dp tells LinearLayout: 'my initial size is nothing, just give me my weighted share'
match_parent with weight is a known performance anti-pattern

questions.title: Blurry Images on High-DPI Device
questions.description: Users on xxhdpi devices report blurry app icons. The app only provides icons in drawable/ (no density qualifier). Fix the resource structure so icons are crisp on all screen densities.

checks.message: Provide high-density versions of the icon
Use correct pixel dimensions for density buckets

questions.explanation: When you put images in drawable/ (no qualifier), Android treats them as mdpi (160dpi, 1x). On an xxhdpi (3x) device, the system scales this 48px image up to 144px — resulting in blur. The fix: provide pre-scaled versions in each density bucket, or use a VectorDrawable (.xml) which renders at perfect quality at any size.
hints: drawable/ without a qualifier is treated as mdpi (baseline density)
Multiply the mdpi size by each density's scale factor

questions.title: Source Set Priority
questions.description: Your app has a strings.xml with app_name defined in both main/res/values/ and debug/res/values/. You're building a debug variant. Which string is used?
options: main — it always has highest priority
debug — build-type source sets overlay main, so same-name resources in debug replace main
The build fails due to a duplicate resource error
It depends on alphabetical order of the source sets
questions.explanation: Source sets have a priority order for resources: build-type + flavor combination > flavor > build type > main. The debug source set overlays main — a resource with the same name and type in debug/ replaces the one in main/. This is how you provide different app names (e.g., "MyApp DEBUG") or API URLs per build type. Note: Java/Kotlin source files are additive (no overriding) — only resources overlay.
hints: Resources in source sets can overlay (replace) each other
Think about why you'd put a different app_name in your debug build

questions.title: Debugging Lifecycle Issues
questions.description: Your app loses user input after the screen rotates, and users report data loss when switching between apps. Describe the tools and developer options you would use to diagnose and fix this.
questions.answer: 1) Enable 'Don't Keep Activities' in Developer Options to simulate process death on every navigation — this reveals state-loss bugs immediately. 2) Add lifecycle logging (Log.d in each callback) to see the exact callback order. 3) Check that essential UI state is saved in SavedStateHandle (survives process death) or at minimum onSaveInstanceState. 4) Verify ViewModel handles configuration changes (rotation). 5) Use Layout Inspector to verify layouts resize correctly. 6) Test rotation with the app in different states.
questions.explanation: Don't Keep Activities is the single most important developer option for finding lifecycle bugs. It forces Activity destruction every time you navigate away, simulating aggressive memory pressure. If your app loses data with this enabled, your state management is broken. ViewModel survives rotation but NOT process death — use SavedStateHandle for the latter. Test with: rotation, Home button, app switcher, split-screen entry/exit.
hints: There's a Developer Option that simulates the worst-case lifecycle scenario
ViewModel survives configuration changes but what survives process death?
